item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hungry Triangles

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of my favourites so far and I'm reading sequentially so to have found one I enjoy so much after 1215 others is quite a thing. Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Keep extradimensional MA weird. I don't fully understand how the first portal works, though I still enjoyed this.

EDIT: I meant for this to be its own post, not a reply.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To cover my own ass for the Credit-A-Thon, just letting you know this image is my own. You can find it on my deviantArt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: you don't need lift to fly. Do rocket thrusters work? Also these things are adorable and I want one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was the one who drew this, by the way. Dr. Berggren and I came up with the idea for this over a facebook chat one day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very interesting, but I think the text needs to streamlined more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since you asked...
...here's the scoop.
  • Not so much the queen. More specifically, it's the "hive" itself. The drones teleport scrap inside it, and the scrap is manufactured into more drones or other things, other kinds of devices and workers. Possibly scrap is also used to give energy to the drones and the hive too. (The drones are, in effect, macroviruses.)
  • It is impossible to generate lift at all. For any device which would normally generate lift, the device would behave as if in a vacuum, even though there is air.
  • Quartz, silicon dioxide, beryllium, some rust.
  • Well, the thing is, they could, but it's a fairly harmless dimension, and they can just as easily observe it from Lenox without getting sunburned.
  • They've sent rovers. Aside from the pyramid there's not much else. Anything that ever was there has been lost to time. I'd say they've covered maybe 500 square kilometers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to ask some nutty questions here:
Is numba 3 (that big pyramid) the "queen" of a hive of these sort of "worker" things that are teleporting scrap back into it?
If not, does that big pyramid effectively reproduce with these things?
(Interestingly one of our defining traits of living is that it must reproduce, meaning that an immortal being that has been without interference and does not die naturally of age would have no need to reproduce and therefore not be living)
What specific property of the atmosphere prevents lift, if any, and could they theoretically haul in another skip or something and just fling shit as high as possible? Or can shit not even be flung in this place?
What is the composition of the sand in this desert-plane?
Have they dispatched a long-term observation team to the desert-plane? (I assume if not that the problem is related to lack of available resources)
Have they dispatched a distance exploration team to the desert-plane? It's not quite clear but it seems they have. How far did this team get?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But if we can't break it apart on a chemical level, how can we even tell that it's a protein?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, no, as is presumed by the Foundation it's a stylized graphic of an instance of SCP-1216. Here's the original design sketch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It doesn't have to have been caused by the earthquake alone: it could just have been, to some degree, coeincidental. Perhaps the earthquake directly affected something which then proceeded to break the wall, or perhaps there was a series of events which weakened the wall, the earthquake being the straw that broke the camel's back.

It might even have just been the unlikely, but not impossible, case that the earthquake and the wall breaking were two wholly unrelated and just happened to occur at about the same time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok, firstly, I really do like this one, and I would love to see some expeditions farther into the desert, just to see how far the desert extends. That said, am I the only one to think that the image alongside the article looks incredibly similar to a stylized graphic of a vagina + ovaries? And I mean significantly similar enough so as to absolutely not be a coincidence. Seriously, is this just me or not?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's a certain juxtaposition to the phrase "extradimensional space adjacent to Lenox, Massachusetts" that just hooks me instantly, and the whole thing is so delightfully odd. +1, good sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You have a strange mind, and I mean that in a good way. Upvoted handily.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually, I think 'adjacent' is a correct term? Just beccause there's only one point where you can cross the boundary doesn't mean they're not necessarily adjacent. It's been a while since I took a topology course, but.. if they physically occupy the same 'space', but are simply seperated by a short distance in another 'direction' (In this case, it would be some some 5th, 6th, 7th, etc dimension), then I think I would consider them adjacent.

Thinking spatially with 4-space-or-higher objects can get tricky, though.
(That said, someone has made a web application that simulates a rubix hypercube. I have never even TRIED to solve it.)

Sort of like laying two blankets on top of each other, with a hole matching up in each, or some such?

(I *AM* a physicists, but my field is nanophysics, so this would be outside my field.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Its existence was first noted shortly after Lenox was affected by the 2011 Virginia earthquake

Speaking as a survivor of the 2011 Virginia earthquake, I can't imagine how something that weak as near to the epicenter as I was would open an interdimensional portal as far away as Lenox.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What is this and why do I like it so much

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Words cannot express how much I want several of these as pets.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can't speak to the first point, but the second and third points have already been answered in this discussion thread :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want several of these as pets, please. They please my eldritch horror sense in the same way bacteriophages do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
extradimensional space adjacent to Lenox, Massachusetts.

I'm not a physicist, but saying an extradimensional space is adjacent to a particular location in our world seems wrong. I think "connected to" or similar would be better.

Except for the bronze spheres, instances of SCP-1216 are composed of an anomalous protein, henceforth pyramitin, which seems to be similar in structure to chitin, except that it has a bulk modulus and density comparable to that of cobalt.

A protein isn't going to be similar to chitin, since chitin is a polymerization of molecules similar to glucose. Chitin is structurally similar to cellulose. If you want it to be similar to a protein that functions similarly to chitin, that'd be keratin.

The expanse is lit by a small, stationary sun located at 48° above the horizon and 66° east from due south,

If the sun is stationary, how do they distinguish east from west? And how do they determine north and south, for that matter?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, if you go in with a GPS it acts like you're still on Earth and gives your coordinates accordingly. That's how they figured out "due south" for the position of the sun.

Glad you like it :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
These things are adorable as hell in all their metal-eating obliviousness. The part about them losing interest when you take away their food, or the way they communicate… Ahhh, I just want to hug their shiny dumb faces.

This, of course, outweighs the somewhat dense initial description. The rest of the article reads well, at least. Oh, and I can't help but laugh at "spatial anomaly adjacent to Lenox, Massachusetts". There's just something ineffably hilarious about an anomaly that specific.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is… cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your chemistry knowledge outstrips mine. *salutes*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're becoming my favourite new writer very quickly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Precisely. To complicate matters, pyramitin blocks some EM radiation (the effect builds up with mass) and doesn't break apart on a chemical level very easily, which makes it difficult to analyze. Sure, we can slice it open and look inside an SCP-1216, but we can't quite disassemble the molecule all the way.

Let's just say that just because our scans can't penetrate SCP-1216-3 doesn't mean there's nothing inside it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe what makes it different from chitin is that it's a protein - I mean, all you need for one is to be a chain of aminoacids - and you can make those by sticking an NH2 and an COOH on ends of just about any carbohydrate

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The science geek in me had to bring this up.

are composed of an anomalous protein, henceforth pyramitin, which seems to be similar in structure to chitin

Chitin is chemically related to cellulose which is a complex carbohydrate, not a protein.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Radioisotopes are almost certainly not what you're looking for — those are only good for tracking things at very close range. Frankly, given how these could manifest literally anywhere where there is scrap metal, I don't see how or why the Foundation could have experimented with tracking individual pieces of scrap metal.

Also, it would be interesting to know how this would react with the Infinite Scrapyard. Anyways, +1 for being delightfully weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's impossible to generate lift at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is certainly… odd. I'm not sure how I feel about it yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: fixinated thank you :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You forgot to change one of the "SCP-XXXX"s to "SCP-1216" towards the end of the containment procedures. Otherwise, no complaints.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Modrons as an SCP, sort of. And I can't find fault with your description either. Squee. Squee. Upvote and squee.

That said, I'd appreciate more info on what exactly prevents flight in there (especially since there are many ways to - propelled baloons , reactive flight, propellers)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What the fuck am I reading?

…in the good way. :D

+1
